entry.title: Run Statutory Reports - Post Tax Payable
entry.content:
Good afternoon!

When accessing the Run Statutory Reports application and, specifically, when executing Model 303 (Spain VAT Return: Model 303), it has been identified in the final step of the process (Post Tax Payable) that the proposal provided by SAP does not correctly recommend the corresponding G/L account.

In the case analyzed, the system suggests account 470* (VAT receivable), whereas it should propose account 475* in those scenarios where the result is an amount payable. It should be noted that, in any case, the amounts are calculated correctly by SAP.

Could you please indicate which configuration point we should review in order to modify the G/L accounts proposed by the system in this final step?

entry.title: Business Configuration related Master Data in Parallel Line
entry.content:
This blog aims to provide information about the new feature that allows copying of Master Data related to Business Configuration in a Parallel Line from the Main Line. Thanks to Divyaprakash Singh & Nicola Pace for valuable inputs.

Manage Check Runs for Business Configurations

Prior to release 2602, the "Manage Check Runs for Business Configurations" app was used to run checks for Business Configurations, displaying the progress and status of the checks. Some configuration tables are designed so that individual fields can reference business master data. For example, when configuring a production plant, a Business Configuration Expert can assign a default supplier that is automatically used when the plant receives materials. This can create a mismatch — i.e., the value stored in the configuration table may reference master data that exists in the Main Line but not in the target tenant (Parallel Line) where the configuration is being deployed. Hence, there is a need to perform these checks to ensure that there is no mismatch between the data referenced in the Business Configuration and the data existing in the tenant.

At the end of the check run, the system issues either a successful or error status:

  • Successful status means there is no missing master data — i.e., all defined references to master data in the scanned configuration tables exist in the current system where the check run was executed.
  • Error status means that some references to master data in the scanned configuration tables do not exist in the current system. Resolution options include replacing the data with master data that exists in the system or manually creating the referenced data in the system.

The recommendation is to perform the checks after executing the Branch, Rebase or Merge process — i.e., when creating a Parallel Line from the Main Line, synchronising the data from Main Line to Parallel line or vice versa.

In addition to check runs, here also possible to perform a simulation of Cross-Client Validation to check configuration consistency using the "Manage Check Runs for Business Configurations" app. This check ensures that changes in configuration data in a tenant of the development system match the configuration used by business data present in target systems, such as test or production systems. The recommendation is to always run the check before you perform a merge operation.

For further details, please refer to the following SAP Help documentation:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/b249d650b15e4b3d9fc2077ee921abd0/3884afb60bfa467892ba620d663620fe.html?version=2602.500

 Copy Master Data from Main Line to Parallel Line for Business Configuration via SAP for Me

As of release 2602, a new feature has been enabled that allows copying of certain Master Data from the Main Line (ML) to the Parallel Line (PL) via SAP for Me. This functionality helps maintain the consistency of Business Configurations by replacing the manual effort needed post branch. Prior to this release, customers had to manually maintain the configuration-referenced master data, the "Manage Check Runs for Business Configurations" app after the Branch process was needs to trigger and configured for the missing master data.

A flag is now available which, when enabled, allows the copy of the following Master Data objects:

  • Address Number
  • Bank Master Record
  • Business Partner
  • Cash Pool
  • Contract Account Header (Partner Specific)
  • Cost Centre
  • Customer
  • FIFM: Financing Code
  • Hierarchy Directory
  • House Bank Account IDs
  • House Banks
  • Job Code Tile (Worker)
  • JVA Agreement
  • JVA Master
  • JVA Partner
  • Material
  • Profit Centre
  • Vendor

Please note: Only during Branch process, the relevant Business Configuration master data is copied from D100 to D120 and from T100 to T120 once the flag is enabled.

It is also important to note that the feature also generates the business user in Parallel Line without any business roles assigned. There are also set of pre-requisites which needs to be met before requesting the copy of master data during branch. More information is available in the following link -

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/b249d650b15e4b3d9fc2077ee921abd0/fe36418d4be944e6a75c8c9d9b1e482e.html

Additional Resources

  • SAP Digital Library for Parallel Line
  • Parallel Line FAQ
  • Branch Your Main Line
  • Parallel Line in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition | SAP Help Portal 
  • Parallel Line in SAP Central Business Configuration
  • SAP S/4HANA Cloud, public edition, release info
  •  Best practices for SAP S/4HANA Cloud, public edition
  • Feature Scope Description
  • What’s New

SAP Community Blogs

  • Introduction to the Parallel Line
  • Parallel Line Branch via SAP for ME
  • Parallel Line as the next evolution of our Public Cloud ERP product journey
  • Rebase: Synchronize the Configurations of Your Main Line with Your Parallel Line

Thank you for reading this blog post.

Your insights and opinions are valuable, so please don't hesitate to contribute by commenting on this blog.

Follow us via @SAP and #S4HANA or follow @JaiSrinivasan .

entry.title: Re: EXCEPTION SEGMENT UNKNOWN RAISED error while calling RFC IDOCTYPE_READ_COMPLETE in Biztalk Middl
entry.content:
The fastest way to make progress here might be to just open IDOCTYPE_READ_COMPLETE in SE37, set an "external breakpoint" for the ABAP user used in BizTalk, and then start the call from BizTalk.

The exception SEGMENT_UNKNOWN could indeed mean what it says (e.g. BizTalk misspelling the segment name or using a wrong input), but I have also seen IDOCTYPE_READ_COMPLETE throwing this error, if the ABAP user had insufficient permissions.... 🙂  (But in that case I would assume, that every segment lookup would fail, not only those for IDocs of release < 7.58? Unless BizTalk uses a different user for these two kinds of IDocs?!)

So if there is only one ABAP user defined in BizTalk, then most probably BizTalk is doing something wrong, like using the E2 name instead of the E1 name, etc. That should then be visible in the ABAP debugger, when inspecting the inputs that BizTalk feeds into IDOCTYPE_READ_COMPLETE.

(The bug that IDOCTYPE_READ_COMPLETE throws SEGMENT_UNKNOWN when in fact the authorizations are missing, has been fixed a long time ago, and as you say, the backend is S/4, it most probably already has that fix. So I think the chances are quite high that BizTalk is doing something wrong here.)

But honestly: this BizTalk version looks very very old! It is still using the classic RFC library, which has been out-of-maintenance since 2016. (Support for Classic RFC Library Ends March 2016 SAP Blogs) I think Microsoft switched from classic RFC Lib to .NET Connector more than 10 years ago. Get the latest version, instead of wasting time with possible bugs that may have been fixed 10 years ago...!

PS: according to Microsoft, the BizTalk SAP Adapter was updated from classic RFC Lib to NCo with release "BizTalk 2013R2 Cumulative Update 2", which came 2015. So you are running a BizTalk version older than 2015. Considering how many bugs have been fixed in BizTalk since then, you may really be wasting your time here using these out-dated components.

entry.title: Re: I am getting a warning message while opening a work o...
entry.content:
What you describing is SAP Assembly/Material Deletion Flag Behavior:

Header Level: Deletion flags on equipment, functional location, or assembly/material trigger immediate validation warnings in IW31/IW32 (Create/Change Maintenance Order). These are warning messages, not blocking, but can be configured using transaction OMCQ.

Component Level: For materials or assemblies added to the components tab of work orders, deletion flag validation is deferred until downstream transactions like MIGO (goods movement) or MRP runs execute. 

Navigation: IW32 > Header Data tab > Reference Object > Assembly

Assembly Defaulting: Work order assemblies automatically default through the Construction Type field ("ConstType") on the Functional Location or Equipment, which references either a direct material number, an assembly with Material BOM, or an Equipment BOM assembly. This construction type configuration links the equipment/location to the appropriate assembly and BOM structure that populates during work order creation.

Hope this is Helpful !

entry.title: Re: EXCEPTION SEGMENT UNKNOWN RAISED error while calling RFC IDOCTYPE_READ_COMPLETE in Biztalk Middl
entry.content:
PS: another idea: as MS BizTalk will reach end-of-life in 2028 anyway, perhaps you can even use a different EDI tool for your IDoc processing needs?

I can recommend SAP Business Connector:

  • It's included for free in any standard SAP license
  • It has very similar functionality as BizTalk (e.g. in this case: receive IDocs from S/4, convert them into some other data format and then forward to some external receiver via HTTP, FTP, Email, JDBC, etc.)
  • You get support for it by simply opening an SAP OSS ticket under component BC-MID-BUS

If you are still at the beginning of your project and not bound to BizTalk for some reason, you could give it a try.
Download is available at
​​https://support.sap.com/sbc-download
​Documentation:
​SAP Business Connector in Detail

entry.title: Eligible Users with Multiple Employments not appearing on VarPay Worksheet at Launch
entry.content:
Hello,

I am seeking clarification on a specific behavior regarding multiple employments within the Variable Pay module, specifically in scenarios where employees have multiple user records (one active and one inactive).

During the worksheet launch process, certain active employees who are eligible based on our plan settings are not being pulled onto the manager's worksheet. However, we have found that we can successfully add these same employees manually using the "Add Employees" tool without encountering any eligibility errors.

Because this is a recurring monthly cycle with a high volume of multiple employment cases, manually adding these employees is not a sustainable workaround.

Is this the expected behavior for Variable Pay templates when dealing with multiple employments?

Are there settings/eligibility changes or better workarounds to ensure the eligible employees automatically appear on worksheets at launch?

Thanks,
Ryan

entry.title: Re: Eligible Users with Multiple Employments not appearin...
entry.content:
Hi @RyanPerkins 

They should be pulled into the worksheet automatically. There must some date conflict or something off in the config or data preventing it.

Things to check here:

1. Do they have a record in Employee History ?

2. Does the UserID tied to the record retrieved have an active Manager in the system ?

3. Is this record correctly tied to Bonus Plans ?

4. Is "Include inactive employees" checked in Plan Setup > Define planners.

Based on your answers above we can explore some more.

Thanks

entry.title: Re: Eligible Users with Multiple Employments not appearin...
entry.content:
1. Yes, they do.

2. Yes, the active/eligible user has an active manager in the system. They appear as eligible under the manager's name in Define Planners (standard hierarchy).

3. Yes, they are tied to a bonus plan.

4. Yes, "Include Inactive Employees" is checked.

entry.title: Re: IBP Exercise: Implicit Activation Prerequisite for Ke...
entry.content:
It should not be required to activate a PA with a stored KF, before referencing it in a calc KF.   

 

Can you post the actual error message from the log?  That will tell you specifically what went wrong and WHERE it went wrong.

If I were to make a guess, you used the out of the box KF for CONSENSUSDEMANDDQTY - which lives at WKPRODLOCCUST by default.  When you created CONSENSUSDEMANDREV @ MTH PROD CUST - you have to create a calculation on the Qty KF that tells the system how to move from WK PROD LOC CUST to MTH PROD CUST.  Then on the Calc Rev KF, did you make the input for CONSENSUSDEMANDQTY at MTH PROD CUST 'calculated'?  Because you are using the calculation within the stored KF, so technically the Qty reference input in the Rev KF, should be 'calculated' even though the KF itself is a stored KF.

Is your planning area based off SAPIBP1 or the HPA (SAPIBP2).  Both of those KF's are standard in the SAPIBP1 and they have different names and planning levels in the HPA. 

So to make a short story long - it is NOT required to activate a stored KF before using it within a calculation. You simply need to ensure you are paying attention to the inputs and whether they are utilizing a stored value or a calculation.

 

Dan

entry.title: Re: Error when deploying UI Integration Card to SuccessFa...
entry.content:
If you are getting simply a HTTP 500 when deploying UI cards to Successfactors, the following may be key:

1) Destination Check: Ensure your sf-api-access destination in BTP has HTML5.DynamicDestination = true.

2) Permissions: Verify the API user in SF has "Manage UI Integration Cards" enabled in their Role.

3) Manual Test: Try a manual ZIP upload in the SF Extension Center; it often reveals the "hidden" error BAS is missing.

Those are just 3 ideas.  Other members in the forum can also try to give their ideas.

Regards,
Mark

entry.title: Introducing Expanded Usage of the Reference Capability Model
entry.content:
A Unified Language Across the SAP Portfolio

What Is the Reference Capability Model?

Over the last years, SAP has developed the SAP Reference Architecture based on Enterprise Architecture methodology. The SAP Reference Architecture consists of the SAP Reference Business Architecture (RBA) and the SAP Reference Solution Architecture (RSA).  A key element of the SAP Reference Architecture is the Reference Capability Model that provides consistent taxonomy. SAP has decided to adopt the Reference Capability Model for a variety of use cases and different tools that go beyond the original Enterprise Architecture related scope where SAP LeanIX and SAP Signavio played the most important role. 

SAP is making a decisive architectural commitment: a transition to a new Reference Capability Model built on a rigorous Enterprise Architecture approach. The goal of the model is both straightforward and far-reaching: establish one unified capability taxonomy across systems, tools, and teams, bringing it all together with a single authoritative structure that speaks the same language everywhere it appears. This is not a housekeeping exercise. It is a deliberate repositioning of how SAP organizes, communicates, and demonstrates value, from the internal processes of portfolio planning and go-to-market execution to the front line of customer engagement and transformation advisory.

At its heart, the model connects two distinct but inseparable layers:

  • Business Capabilities: A structured, hierarchical map of business functions and activities, grounded in real enterprise processes and deliberately aligned with how customers think about — and describe — their own organizations. This layer provides shared vocabulary for every business-level conversation, from strategic planning to process transformation.
  • Solution Capabilities: The corresponding SAP products and solutions that enable those business capabilities, clearly linked, consistently named, and governed across the entire portfolio. Each solution capability is traceable to the business capability it serves, creating an unambiguous, auditable line of sight between customer need and SAP solution.

Together, these two layers provide a single, governed bridge between what a customer needs to achieve and the SAP solutions designed to help them get there. The connection between business intent and technology enablement is no longer implicit or approximate: it is explicit, navigable, and consistent at every level of abstraction.

This directly supports SAP’s broader Enterprise Architecture vision: systematically guiding customers from their business challenges to the most relevant SAP solutions, at every stage of their journey. Rather than presenting the SAP portfolio as a flat catalogue of features and modules, the Reference Capability Model allows to easily identify the relevant SAP solutions based on business requirements and helps EAs to guide through the Transformation. Conversations begin with what customers are trying to accomplish — reducing days-sales-outstanding, accelerating procurement cycles, improving supply chain resilience — and the capability model provides the structured pathway from that business intent to the specific SAP products and features that enable it.

Critically, this design also ensures that the model scales across every context in which SAP engages with customers. Whether a conversation is happening in a boardroom during an enterprise transformation scoping session, in a Signavio workshop tracing process flows to solution coverage, or in an automated recommendation engine surfacing the most relevant solution for a given scenario, the same capability structures apply, consistently and without ambiguity. The discipline established at the foundation is precisely what makes that consistency possible.

In Which Tools Will It Be Utilized?

The Reference Capability Model will be embedded across SAP’s core customer-facing and internal tools, with adoption expanding progressively. In each tool, capabilities are surfaced in the way most natural to that tool’s workflow, ensuring that the unified taxonomy enhances, rather than disrupts, existing user experience patterns. Here are some examples of how the new Reference Capability Model affects the tools:

  • SAP LeanIX: Business Capabilities have served since 2024 as a Reference Catalog, giving Enterprise Architects a governed, portfolio-aligned set of building blocks for their application landscapes. Solution Capabilities are surfaced as suggested SAP solutions, empowering architects to make informed, capability-driven technology decisions grounded in the full SAP portfolio rather than ad hoc product knowledge.
  • SAP Signavio Process Navigator. Exposes the full Capability Hierarchy alongside Solution Capability details and their mapping to S/4HANA Best Practices Scope Items, giving customers an end-to-end picture of how SAP solutions relate to their processes. Consultants and customers can navigate from a business capability directly to the scope items and best practices that implement it.
  • SAP Signavio Process Insights: Solution Capabilities power intelligent recommendations, ensuring that every insight generated from process mining data is anchored to a relevant, concrete SAP solution. This closes the loop between process performance visibility and actionable improvement guidance.
  • SAP Signavio Value Accelerators: The Reference Capability Model is also used in SAP Signavio Value Accelerator packages to ensure consistent taxonomy along the transformation toolchain.
  • SAP Road Map Explorer: Innovations are structured around Business Capabilities, with Solution Capabilities shown as attributes of each innovation, providing a capability-driven view of SAP’s product direction. This makes it straightforward for customers to understand which planned innovations are relevant to their specific business capability landscape. For more information on the transition of SAP Road Map Explorer, check this blog.
  • SAP Solution Scout: Solution Capabilities appear at the most granular level of the Industry Value Map, making it easier to pinpoint the right solution for a specific business scenario. The result is a more precise, capability-anchored discovery experience that reduces time-to-recommendation for both customers and SAP account teams.
  • SAP Value Lifecycle Manager: Solution Capabilities are assigned to Value Drivers, ensuring that value realization conversations are grounded in capability-level evidence rather than high-level abstractions. This enables more credible, measurable business cases tied directly to the SAP solutions a customer has deployed or is planning to deploy.

What Are the Benefits for Customers?

The Reference Capability Model is available for many different industries. While it is provided by SAP, it is important to note that the Business Capabilities are modeled purely from a business perspective and not from an SAP application perspective. The model contains even Business Capabilities, for which SAP does not offer solutions. The mapping to the SAP applications is done systematically through the Solution Capabilities as described above.

Customers have the freedom to decide how to leverage it for their own customer specific capability model as part of their Enterprise Architecture practice. Some customers regard the Reference Capability Model as industry leading practice and adopt it to a full or very large extent. In any case does the Reference Capability Model significantly accelerate the creation of a customer specific capability model.

A high-quality capability model provides numerous advantages:

  • A business capability map serves as a framework to help you understand the resources needed to run your business. It enables you to evaluate how well your existing technology is aligned with your business needs.
  • Mapping applications to business capabilities will reveal gaps and redundancies in your IT landscape. In this way, you can uncover opportunities for consolidation, elimination, or modernization.
  • Business capability mapping of your target state is also critical for business transformation. In order to support new business capabilities, you must determine how your IT landscape needs to evolve.

The Reference Capability Model is not simply a new taxonomy; it is a practical tool with tangible outcomes for customers, enterprise architects, and SAP teams at every stage of the engagement lifecycle. The following benefits reflect both the immediate advantages of a unified capability structure and the longer-term value that accrues as adoption deepens across tools and teams:

  • A shared business language.  Customers and SAP teams now operate from the same capability taxonomy, fully aligned with the SAP portfolio. This eliminates ambiguity in conversations about needs, gaps, and solutions — replacing the informal, often inconsistent vocabulary that previously varied from team to team and tool to tool. When a customer describes a capability, SAP can immediately map it to the right solution; when SAP proposes a solution, the customer can immediately understand which of their business capabilities it addresses.
  • Streamlined customer experience.  Consistent capability structures across different touchpoints mean customers receive coherent, connected guidance for their journey. There are no jarring transitions between tools that use different terms for the same concept, and no need to re-establish context as a conversation moves from discovery to design to value realization.
  • Full transparency into software adoption.  With capabilities mapped end-to-end from business need to product, customers gain clear visibility into which capabilities are covered by their current landscape, where gaps exist, and what the path forward looks like. This supports more confident, evidence-based decisions about investment priorities and transformation sequencing.
  • Empowered RISE Enterprise Architects.  A structured, unified framework makes it significantly easier to bridge business and IT conversations — accelerating alignment and reducing time-to-value for transformation programs. Enterprise Architects gain a reliable, governed reference structure that supports both strategic planning and detailed solution design, without the need to maintain their own custom capability catalogues.

The Reference Capability Model represents a foundational shift in how SAP connects business intent to technological outcomes. It resolves a structural challenge that has persisted across the portfolio for years, replacing fragmented, tool-local definitions with a single, authoritative, and governed taxonomy that spans tool, team, and customer conversations. As adoption deepens across tools and teams throughout 2026, the model will become an essential asset for every customer, architect, and partner navigating the SAP portfolio, not as one more framework to learn, but as the common language that makes every SAP engagement more precise, more connected, and more valuable.

More Information

To explore how the Business Capabilities and the Solution Capabilities of the Reference Capability model relate to the legacy capabilities, refer to the Capability Mapping Excel.

For an introduction to Reference Business Architecture (RBA) and Reference Solution Architecture (RSA) please see the following Blogs:

  • https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-architecture-blog-posts/what-s-new-in-sap-reference-architecture-spring-edition/ba-p/14087513
  • https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-architecture-blog-posts/sap-reference-architecture-content-an-overview/ba-p/11055
  • https://community.sap.com/t5/enterprise-architecture-blog-posts/sap-reference-architecture-content-an-overview-part-2/ba-p/216728
  • https://community.sap.com/t5/blog-posts-about-sap-websites/sap-road-map-explorer-leverages-the-new-reference-capability-model/ba-p/14380387
  • https://www.leanix.net/en/sap-leanix-business-capability-map

entry.title: yaai - ABAP AI tools - Evolving Toward Agentic AI
entry.content:
yaai - ABAP AI tools - Evolving Toward Agentic AI

Just like every other AI tool and framework, ABAP AI tools have been evolving toward agentic AI. Over the past months, numerous features have been added, and two companion projects have been created to extend its capabilities in an optional manner, allowing you to choose what interests you and install it in your ABAP system.

ABAP AI tools now offer the option to configure AI agents by assigning system instructions, tools, and supporting documentation. Special tools have been added to enable models to load tools and documents on demand, avoiding context overload with unnecessary information. Additionally, two other groups of tools were introduced to allow agents to create plans and documents. The planning tools are particularly helpful for agent memory and for users to analyze intended actions, which can depend on approval if instructed accordingly.

Setting up AI agents in ABAP AI tools is relatively straightforward but requires maintaining some tables. Testing agents becomes more complex as conversations grow and agents begin calling tools, making it difficult to analyze their behavior. To simplify configuration and testing, a SAPUI5 cockpit was developed: the ABAP AI Tools Cockpit (detailed in the Companion Projects section below).

The next step in advancing toward agentic AI was developing tools for agents to perform actions in the system via function calling. As an ABAP developer working with a SAP AS ABAP Developer Edition system, the natural choice was to create tools for managing technical objects such as DDIC objects, message classes, translations, and transport requests. These tools were placed in a separate repository, making them optional. The following sections provide more information about these companion projects.

Companion Projects

ABAP AI tools Cockpit

The ABAP AI Tools Cockpit is a SAPUI5 application that serves as the user interface for configuring and testing AI agents using ABAP AI tools. In the Cockpit, you can set up AI agents by assigning system instructions and the tools they should have access to. Afterward, you can test the agent using the integrated chat to interact with it. In the chat view (separate from the integrated chat), you can inspect and monitor all messages and tools called by the agent. All interactions with the AI agent are visualized in a sequence diagram, making it easy to analyze the conversation flow and actions executed by the agent using the provided tools.

 

 

More about ABAP AI tools Cockpit

How to Create and Test AI Agents in the ABAP AI tools Cockpit

ABAP AI tools Function Calling Library

This project contains numerous tools (ABAP class methods) that AI agents can use to interact with the ABAP system. These tools do not cover all features of the objects they handle. The goal is to provide some basic yet useful functionalities, enabling the creation of AI agents that deliver value. This allows experimentation with AI agent development and lets AI agents handle some of the “boring stuff” we need to do from time to time, like creating DDIC objects or managing translations.

More about function calling in ABAP AI tools

More about ABAP AI tools Function Calling Library

Available Tools

Here are some of the available tools.

ABAP Dictionary (DDIC) Management
DDIC Objects

  • Domains (ycl_aai_fc_domain_tools)

    • Create, update, and delete domains
    • Manage fixed values
    • Manage translations for domain fixed values

  • Data Elements (ycl_aai_fc_data_element_tools)

    • Create, update, and delete data elements
    • Manage translations for data element labels

  • Structures (ycl_aai_fc_structure_tools)

    • Create, update, and delete structures

  • Tables (ycl_aai_fc_table_tools)

    • Create, update, and delete transparent tables
    • Manage technical settings (data class, size category)

  • Table Types (ycl_aai_fc_table_type_tools)

    • Create, update, and delete table types
Other objects

  • Message Classes (ycl_aai_fc_message_class_tools)

    • Create and manage message classes
    • Add, update, and delete messages
    • Handle message translations across languages

  • Text Pools (ycl_aai_fc_text_pool_tools)

    • Manage text symbols for programs/reports
    • Manage translations for text symbols and selection texts
Transport Management
  • Transport Requests (ycl_aai_fc_transport_tools)
    • Create workbench and customizing requests
    • Search and read transport request details

Explore, Experiment, and Contribute

These tools are free to use, open source, and available to you on GitHub.

Explore the repositories on GitHub:

  • yaai - ABAP AI tools: https://github.com/christianjianelli/yaai
  • yaai - ABAP AI tools Cockpit: https://github.com/christianjianelli/yaai_cockpit
  • yaai - ABAP AI tools Function Calling Tools: https://github.com/christianjianelli/yaai_fc

entry.title: SAP Joule Document Grounding: What I Learned the Hard Way
entry.content:
I'll be honest: when I was asked to set up SAP Joule Document Grounding in SuccessFactors for our HR business, I was excited but also a little nervous. It was my first time working on SAP's AI capabilities, and the idea of connecting Joule to our actual company documents felt like a big step. The official documentation made it look straightforward enough. Spoiler: it wasn't. 😁
What followed was a week of troubleshooting, a lot of curl errors, a few moments of genuine confusion, and eventually, a working setup. This post is my attempt to save you some of that time.

The Issues I Hit (and How I Fixed Them):

Runtime Environment: pick "Other", not Cloud Foundry

First mistake I made was selecting Cloud Foundry as the runtime when creating the Document Grounding service instance. 😅It looks like the natural choice if you're used to working on BTP, but it generates the wrong type of credentials: the mTLS endpoint you need simply won't be there.

Fix: Always choose Other for both the Document Grounding instance and the Cloud Identity Services instance. Yes, BTP will warn you that it can't bind natively in CF... that's fine, ignore it.

Windows curl will not work. Full stop.

This one cost me a few hours. I kept running into this:

curl: (58) schannel: Failed to import cert file doc-grounding.crt

The curl.exe that comes with Windows uses the Schannel TLS library, which simply doesn't handle PEM-format certificates the way we need it to. I tried converting to PFX, I tried PowerShell's Invoke-WebRequest, nothing worked cleanly. 

Fix: Download a portable curl build with OpenSSL from curl.se/windows . No installation needed, just unzip it somewhere and always call it by its full path:

C:\curl\bin\curl.exe --cert doc-grounding.crt --key doc-grounding.key ...

Once I switched to this, the certificate errors disappeared completely.

The certificate files have "\n" instead of real line breaks.

After saving the certificate and key from the BTP service binding JSON, I assumed they were ready to use. They weren't. The values in the JSON use \n as a literal escape sequence,  not actual newlines. A PEM file with \n in it isn't a valid PEM file.

Fix: Run this in PowerShell after saving each file:

(Get-Content -Path doc-grounding.crt -Raw) -replace '\\n', "`n" |
    Set-Content -Path doc-grounding.crt -Force

Do the same for the .key file. After this, -----BEGIN CERTIFICATE----- will be on its own line, as it should be.

PowerShell adds an invisible BOM to your JSON files.

This one is particularly sneaky because you can't see it:

{"error": {"code": 400, "message": "Unexpected UTF-8 BOM"}}

PowerShell's Out-File adds a UTF-8 Byte Order Mark by default, and the Document Grounding API doesn't like it. The fix is to write files differently:

[System.IO.File]::WriteAllText(
    "C:\path\to\body.json",
    '{"type":"MSSharePoint","configuration":{...}}',
    [System.Text.Encoding]::ASCII
)

Then pass it to curl with --data "@C:\path\to\body.json".

Asterisks in cron expressions will break PowerShell.

If you try to pass a cron expression like 0 * * * * directly in a PowerShell command, things go sideways fast, PowerShell interprets * as a wildcard. The error messages you get are confusing and have nothing to do with cron.
Fix: Write the full JSON body to a file (see the previous point) instead of passing it inline. This sidesteps the wildcard issue entirely.

401 from Microsoft Graph: check three things.

If the pipeline creation goes through but you get 401 errors when it actually tries to read from SharePoint, there are three things to check in Azure AD:

  • The permission (Sites.Read.All) must be Application type, not Delegated
  • Admin Consent must be granted tenant-wide: per-user consent is not enough for a service account
  • The Client ID in the BTP Destination must be the Application (client) ID from the app's Overview page, not the Secret ID (which is a completely different GUID shown in Certificates & secrets)

That last one tripped us up, the Secret ID and the Client ID look the same format but are completely different values.

SharePoint's hidden libraries will get indexed and your users can't open them.

This one surprised me. Users were getting answers from Joule that referenced documents they couldn't open. "Access Denied." Not a great experience.
What was happening: when you don't specify includePaths, the pipeline indexes everything in the SharePoint site, including system libraries that are hidden from regular users. The most problematic one we encountered was the Preservation Hold Library (/PreservationHoldLibrary), a Microsoft 365 compliance feature that stores retained copies of modified or deleted documents. Users can't see it in SharePoint, but the indexing service account can and does.
My first thought was to add an exclusion list. Unfortunately, the Document Grounding API doesn't have an excludePaths parameter. You can only specify what to include.

A Limitation Worth Knowing Before You Go Live
I want to be upfront about something that isn't obvious from the documentation and has real implications for how you design your deployment.
Document Grounding does not respect individual user permissions in SharePoint.
Here's how it actually works: the pipeline uses a system-level service account to index documents. All that content goes into a shared vector store. When any user asks Joule a question, Joule searches that shared store, it doesn't check whether that specific user has permission to read those documents in SharePoint.
In practice, this means: if a document is indexed, every Joule user can potentially receive its content in an answer, regardless of what their SharePoint permissions say.
For our HR use case, this was something we had to think carefully about. If your SharePoint site contains documents with different access levels — say, management-only documents in the same library as general staff documents — Document Grounding will treat them all the same.
What you can do about it:

Use includePaths to only index folders where every document is appropriate for every Joule user — this is the most practical control available right now
Consider creating a dedicated SharePoint site that contains only content approved for all staff, rather than indexing your main site
Before adding any folder to your pipeline, ask yourself: "Would I be comfortable if any Joule user saw this?"

This is a known architectural constraint of the current version. Joule Document Grounding in SuccessFactors - Role-Based Access Control at Retrieval Level 

Fix: Be explicit with includePaths from the start. List only the folders that contain documents your users are actually allowed to open. Yes, it's a bit more work to maintain, but it prevents this whole category of problem.

The Tool That Made Everything Easier.

After going through all of the above, I realized there was another gap: there is currently no SAP-provided UI to monitor your Document Grounding pipelines. You can create a pipeline and walk away, but if you want to check its status, see which documents were indexed, or trigger a manual run, you have two options: call the REST API directly using a tool like Bruno (a great open-source API client), or build something yourself.
I went with the second option and built a PowerShell interactive menu script to manage the whole Document Grounding lifecycle without having to remember curl commands and JSON structures every time.

The script handles all the certificate and token complexity automatically, you just pick a number. A few things I found particularly useful in practice:
Option 5 — Get Pipeline Documents fetches the full list of indexed documents with automatic pagination, shows a breakdown by status (SUCCESS / FAILED / SKIPPED), and can export everything to a JSON file on your desktop. This is invaluable for verifying what actually got indexed after a pipeline run.
Option 8 — Create Pipeline comes pre-loaded with our production includePaths list, so there's no risk of accidentally indexing the wrong folders when setting up a new pipeline.
Option 9 — Trigger uses the correct endpoint and body format we discovered through all the troubleshooting above.

It was a fun challenge overall 🙂 my first proper dive into SAP's AI capabilities, and definitely not the last. If you're setting up Document Grounding and hit something not covered here, drop a comment. Happy to help.

entry.title: G/L Bank Accounts - posting without entering house bank and Account ID?
entry.content:
Hi SAP experts!

Whenever I want to enter a posting to a bank account, I have to enter the house bank and account ID in the G/L account item every time. As my customer only has one bank account and makes manual postings to it quite frequently, I was wondering if it’s possible to automate this entry or omit it altogether. To this end, I’ve tried and found out the following:

1. Substitution: substitution only works for account types D or K. It is presumably intended for invoices, so that the system knows which house bank to use to pay an invoice.

2. Field status: I had hoped that the two fields ‘House Bank’ and ‘Account ID’ would be declared as mandatory fields in the field status group of the bank account’s G/L account, and that I could simply change them to optional fields. However, the fields do not appear at all in the field status settings.

Is there an alternative that would save my client from having to enter these fields manually?

Thank you in advance for any hint.

Best regards,
Rado

AI Adoption & Activation – Getting the Most from SuccessFactors AI

Join us for a three-part interactive discussion series designed for SuccessFactors customers who want to move beyond simply switching AI on — and start unlocking real business value.

Together with SAP AI subject matter experts and transformation leaders, we’ll explore what truly drives AI success in 2026 — from first activation through to scaled, sustainable adoption.

The Series Includes:

Session One: From Activation to Early Adoption
Laying the right foundations — moving beyond “turning it on” and into practical, everyday use.

Session Two: Driving Adoption and Demonstrating Business Value
Embedding AI into workflows, enabling your people, and measuring impact where it matters most.

Session Three: Scaling, Governance, and the Future of HR
Building the right frameworks to scale responsibly, manage risk, and future-proof your HR strategy.

Each session is designed to help you maximize the value of SuccessFactors AI within your organization and accelerate meaningful, measurable adoption.

Special note: Including guest speaker and partner perspectives, sharing real-life experiences, lessons learned, and practical insights from their AI journeys.

entry.title: Re: Flexible workflow for sales documents multiple curren...

Dear Leonardo Delgado,

When you create a sales document that has a different currency than the one from the condition in the app Manage Sales Document Workflows, the system converts the amount to the sales order currency and then evaluates.

In case you are interested, below is the call stack and snippet of code of when the conversion happens:

"

21CALL FUNCTION 'CONVERT_TO_LOCAL_CURRENCY'

22  EXPORTING

23    date             = sy-datum

24    foreign_amount   = lv_amount

25    foreign_currency = iv_currency_in

26    local_currency   = iv_currency_out

27      IMPORTING

28    local_amount     = rv_amount <<<——This is the converted amount that will be used to evaluate the net value condition

29  EXCEPTIONS

30    no_rate_found    = 1

31    overflow         = 2

32    no_factors_found = 3

33    no_spread_found  = 4

34    derived_2_times  = 5

35    OTHERS           = 6.

36

25METHODEVALUATECL_SWF_FLEX_RUN_CONDITION=====CPCL_SWF_FLEX_RUN_CONDITION=====CCIMP62

entry.title: Budget Availability Control for WBS Element in PR/PO – How to Enforce Hard Stop in SAP GROW
entry.content:
Hello Experts,

I am currently working on a procurement scenario integrated with Project System in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

Scenario:

We are using WBS Element (Project System) as account assignment in Purchase Requisition (PR) and Purchase Order (PO).
Budget has been maintained at WBS level.
PR and PO are created with account assignment category "P" (Project).
Issue:
When creating PR or PO, the system does NOT trigger any budget-related validation.

Expected Behavior:
We expect the system to:

At least trigger a warning message when the budget is exceeded, OR
Ideally trigger a hard error (blocking) when PR/PO value exceeds the available WBS budget
Current Behavior:

PR and PO can be created successfully even when exceeding WBS budget
No warning message is triggered
No error or blocking occurs at all
System does not seem to perform any budget availability check
Questions:

Is budget availability control (warning or error) supported for WBS elements in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition?
Are there any required configurations to activate budget checks for WBS during PR/PO creation?
Is this limitation by design in Public Cloud?
What is the recommended best practice to enforce budget control in this scenario?
Custom Logic (BAdI)?
Workflow-based validation?
Alternative object like Internal Order?
Additional Info:

Scope Item: 4RF – Enterprise Portfolio and Project Management
Procurement integrated with Project (PR/PO → WBS)
Budget is already maintained in WBS and visible in reporting
Any guidance or best practice would be highly appreciated.

Thank you.

entry.title: Re: After deleting tiles catalog and group, and subsequen...
entry.content:
Hello,

When a tile is removed from a custom catalog in the SAP Fiori Launchpad, the corresponding target mapping and OData service authorizations may still remain in the PFCG role associated with the catalog. 
This is because removing a tile from a catalog does not automatically update the role in PFCG to reflect the change. 
The synchronization between the catalog and the PFCG role menu is not automatic, and manual adjustments are required to ensure consistency.

Correct Procedure to Manage This Scenario:

1.Review the PFCG Role Menu: 
After removing a tile from the custom catalog, navigate to the PFCG role associated with the catalog. 
Check the role menu to identify any remaining entries related to the removed tile.

2.Manually Remove Target Mappings and OData Services: 
Delete the target mappings and OData service authorizations related to the removed tile from the PFCG role menu. 
This ensures that the role does not grant access to the removed tile or its associated services.
Following KBA for your reference as well:
2799473 - How to delete (remove) an OData service from service catalog (/IWFND/MAINT_SERVICE)

3.Clean up the cache:
Run cache clearing as per note 231949    How to clean up the cache after applying changes that affect SAP Fiori apps 
to ensure that the system reflects the updated catalog and role configuration. 
This step helps resolve any inconsistencies caused by outdated data [1]

4.Verify Role Synchronization: 
After making the changes, verify that the PFCG role menu accurately reflects the updated catalog content. 
Ensure that the removed tile and its associated services are no longer accessible to users assigned to the role.

5.Test the Changes: 
Log in to the SAP Fiori Launchpad with a user assigned to the updated role and confirm that the removed tile is no longer visible or accessible.

If you have further question please feel free to let me know.

Best regards,
Joe

entry.title: Agentic coding: does it really work, or is it just a skill issue on my side?
entry.content:
So I’ve been working lately with different coding agents, and I’ve noticed something interesting.

As projects grow, even when I improve the context, guardrails, specs, hooks, and context-management techniques, I still sometimes see glitches in the code:

* Files that were not supposed to be touched get modified.
* Previously working features get deleted.
* New issues get introduced.

To be clear, these don’t reach production. We still review changes, run tests, and promote carefully through higher environments.

But we do spend time finding and fixing these issues, whether by re-prompting the agent, making the changes ourselves, or using a mix of both.

So I’m curious:

Has anyone else experienced this with coding agents?

Or, as people often say on social media, is this just a skill issue on my side? 😂
I’m still learning, so I’m genuinely open to that possibility.

Being used to a bit more precision at work, this has left me a bit confused and, honestly, a little anxious haha.

Would love to hear how others are handling this.

Thanks for sharing your experience 🙂

entry.title: When One Release Has to Reach Many SAP Cloud Integration Tenants
entry.content:
Sharing an internal tool we built for multi-tenant delivery orchestration — and asking whether other teams face the same problem.
If this resonates with enough people, I am open to releasing the source or making this available as a BTP SaaS — but first I want to know whether others face the same problem.

Show the Solution First

The fastest way to explain the solution is to show the interface - One view shows the overview, one view shows the fan-out release across source and target tenants, and one view validates whether a tenant is ready before the first delivery starts. Together, these screens make it clear that the solution is an operator workflow for delivery coordination, not just another technical utility.

When Delivery Becomes a Fan-Out Problem

In our landscape, one release can affect 15 production instances and more than 100 integration artifacts. Before orchestration, a multi-instance production release meant 30+ manual actions across several SAP tools, close to one hour of operator work, and no single place to see the full flow.
 
That is the point where delivery stops being a simple deploy step. It becomes a coordination problem.

Turning Scattered Steps into One Governed Flow

SAP Cloud Transport Management(TMS) already handled transport and routing. Content Agent Service(CAS) works for exporting MTAR files, i.e., automatically generating Transport Requests. SAP Cloud Integration already handled deployment and runtime operations. The gap was the operator workflow across many targets.
 
To close that gap, we built an internal orchestration layer we call Delivery Orchestrator for Cloud Integration. It coordinates request creation, route-aware target calculation, batch import, batch deploy, centralized status, approvals, notifications, and traceability in one place. It does not replace SAP tools. It makes them usable as one governed release flow.
 
For our team, that changed one multi-instance release from 30+ manual actions and about one hour into a 4-step flow that takes about five minutes. Just as importantly, approval and auditability moved into the workflow instead of being handled around it.
The clearest way to understand that change is the Delivery Flow view itself. One screen shows the source tenant, the target tenants, the transport path, and the import and deploy state together. For an operator, that is the difference between understanding a release and chasing it across multiple tools.

Bootstraper - Reliable Fan-Out Starts Before the First Import

Once fan-out delivery became easier, another weakness became impossible to ignore: tenant bootstrap. Connecting a new SAP Cloud Integration tenant still depended on destinations, service instances, authentication settings, and other BTP prerequisites being configured correctly. The hard part was not the manual setup itself. The hard part was that misconfiguration often remained invisible until the first real import or deploy failed.
 
That changes the meaning of bootstrap. In a one-to-many delivery model, bootstrap is not just onboarding. It is part of delivery readiness.
 
This is why guided tenant bootstrap and prerequisite validation became an important extension of the orchestration story for us. The goal is to inspect what is missing, make setup visible, automate what can be done safely, and surface what still requires manual governance. Instead of discovering a wrong URL, missing token, or incomplete service setup during runtime delivery, the operator can see those gaps before the first release starts.
 

A Secondary Capability: Version Compare

Bootstrap is the more important next step for us, because reliable onboarding is what makes the product safer to adopt across teams. But once many tenants are active, another operational question quickly matters as well: what is actually different across them?
 
That is where Version Compare becomes useful as a secondary capability. It gives operators a cross-tenant view of design-time and runtime artifact versions, highlights mismatches, and makes it easier to see where source and target tenants are no longer aligned. It is not the main story of this article, and it does not matter more than bootstrap. But it is still valuable because operational reliability is not only about moving releases forward. It is also about spotting drift early enough to act on it. 

 

A Question to the Community

We are sharing this pattern now because the core flow is already live and internally validated. The goal is not to announce a finished product, but to learn whether other SAP Cloud Integration teams face the same combination of fan-out delivery, bootstrap fragility, and cross-tenant drift.

If you operate across multiple tenants, I would be very interested to hear:
  • how you coordinate one release across many targets today
  • how much manual work still sits between transport and deployment
  • whether tenant bootstrap or prerequisite validation becomes a bottleneck in your landscape
  • how you currently see version drift across tenants

entry.title: Unexpected plugin behaviour of Data collection plugins in POD 2.0 framework in SAP DM
entry.content:
Hi Community Members,

We are currently working with Data Collection related plugins in POD 2.0 and have observed several inconsistencies and limitations in the Data Collection Group table and POD behavior. I would like to highlight these points to understand whether this is standard behavior, a configuration gap, or a limitations of new 2.0 framework.

Below are the detailed observations.

1. Postings Button – No Clear Purpose

In the Data Collection Group table, a Postings button is available.

However:

  • The button does not seem to have any functional significance.
  • There is no mention or explanation of this button in the SAP Help Documentation.
  • Clicking the button does not result in any meaningful or expected action (at least from a functional standpoint).

This raises questions such as:

  • What is the intended purpose of the Postings button?

  • Is it meant for a future enhancement, or is it currently unused?

2. Collect Button Enabled Even When Multiple Data Collection = False

We observed that:

  • The Collect button is enabled even for Data Collection Groups where:
    • Multiple Data Collection = False
    • Data has already been collected one time
  • The button behavior is not configurable
  • From a usability point of view, the button should ideally be:
    • Disabled, or
    • Hidden once data collection is completed for non‑multiple data collection groups (the usual POD 1.0 behaviour)

This can be misleading for users and may result in unnecessary button clicks and errors.


3. Unable to View Collected Parameters from POD

Another issue we are facing:

  • After data collection is completed, the collected parameters are not visible from the POD
  • There seems to be no direct way for users to:
    • Review collected values
    • Verify what was captured during data collection ( Which we used to do in POD 1.0)

Clarification needed:

  • Is there a standard POD plugin or configuration required to display collected parameters?

  • Or is this a current limitation of the framework?

4. Data Collection Groups Refresh Action Not getting Triggered via Custom action Button

We attempted to improve usability by:

  • Assigning the Data Collection Refresh action to a custom button where we are collecting data using a production process design in the POD

However:

  • Clicking the custom button does trigger the PPD but not the refresh action (confirmed this by checking in Network tab as well, where we didn't saw and call happened for DC groups refresh)
  • The action works only when using standard/default "save button" that they have given along with the Workcenter POD template in the Data collection parameter plugin.

This limits flexibility in POD customization and user‑driven refresh behavior.

 

Are these known limitations or expected system behaviors?

Are there recommended workarounds or best practices?

Is there any roadmap or note addressing these issues?

Any guidance or insights from SAP experts or peers would be greatly appreciated.

Thanks in advance.
Sudheer Manepalli

entry.title: Re: Error AAPO105
entry.content: No, you cannot enable valuation area 12 only for one AUC asset. It is either global (AFBN) or you create a new asset master with the correct areas. Having said that the Best Practice is if valuation area 12 is required for reporting or statutory purposes, the clean approach is to activate it globally via AFBN. If it is only needed for a subset of assets, then the safer path is to create new assets with the correct valuation areas and transfer values.

entry.title: Re: Creation of New PO condition Type
entry.content: Create a new condition type in customizing, mark it as statistical and set an Accrual Key, so it does not change the material price, add it to the PO pricing schema, and then assign a separate G/L account in account determination. This way, the PO condition posts independently to its own account without affecting inventory valuation.

entry.title: GRC Tuesdays: What's New in SAP Watch List Screening – Delta Screening & Streamlined Investigation
entry.content:
With advance in technology, also comes increased expectations from regulatory bodies for companies to rapidly adapt to changing circumstances. For instance, when it comes to sanctioned party screening, authorities now prioritize real-time compliance, contextual analysis and enhanced due diligence processes over ad-hoc list-matching.

To continuously support organizations in this area, multiple enhancements have once again been delivered to SAP Watch List Screening.

In this short blog, I have selected a few, but you will also find more details in the dedicated section of the SAP Help Portal: What’s New in SAP Watch List Screening

 

Delta Screening

 

If there is no predetermined timetable for sanction lists update but rather names and entities being added or removed by authorities as necessary and appropriate, one thing is sure: this is quite frequent with some lists being modified as regularly as daily. Not only to add or remove records, but to include additional details, aliases, updated addresses, etc.

And, if experts assess that the percentage of these updates is limited (some claim less than 1% of total records are updated daily), this still means a great volume of changes that organizations need to remain compliant with.

To do so, compliance departments can opt for 2 options:

  • Systematic full rescreening of all records and entire sanction list set => this is the “bullet proof” approach – or at least as close as it gets of course, but equates in practice to a new full screening at every run so it is heavy and not efficient for ongoing monitoring
  • Delta screening with targeted compliance measures that re-check only newly added or updated watch list records for potential sanction hits => with this approach, Compliance Officers benefit from faster, more focused checks, saving time and ensuring that newly added or updated data is promptly screened without missing potential risks

Full screening was already available in SAP Watch List Screening since the very inception of this service where documents were re-checked upon changes and further processing and business partners had an own scheduling report for full re-screenings.

Delta Screening has been added in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Edition 2602 and SAP S/4HANA On-Premise release 2025 FPS2. With Delta Screening, the changes in watch lists are monitored and trigger a screening re-run on customer information - request status, business partners, and documents.

Allow me to illustrate this with a simple example from the SAP Help Portal section for this enhancement:

A business partner is screened on creation, resulting in no hits. Therefore, the business partner is released. Some days later, a new watch list version is uploaded containing new watch list entries. SAP Watch List Screening now automatically screens the business partner against these new watch list entries and finds a hit and blocks the business partner. The next postprocessing run fetches this update and blocks the business partner in the system accordingly. In doing so, the screening status of the business partner (as well as the documents) stays up-to-date with watch list changes, ensuring consistency and compliance.

With delta screening, companies can:

 

 

Audit Trail

 

Full auditability of sanction party list screenings is often a legal requirement within sanction regulations. For instance, under 31 CFR Part 501 - Records and recordkeeping requirements, the U.S. Office of Foreign Assets Control (OFAC) mandates that organizations “keep a full and accurate record of each such transaction engaged in”.

Complete logs of who performed the screening, what was screened, and when it took place help businesses accomplish just that: demonstrate compliance, manage liability, and foster trust by showing that processes are transparent and defensible.

To support this requirement, an new Fiori app has been released: Display Audit Trail.

With this app organizations – or their auditors – can display an audit trail for their screening requests and navigate to individual screening requests for further investigation:

 

 

Display Watch Lists

 

Following on the topic of audit, you may recall that we previously mentioned in a What’s new in SAP Watch List Screening blog the availability of Custom Watch Lists that organizations can upload in addition to the provided list.

Whether it be for predefined provided lists or custom lists, auditors, but also compliance managers and screening specialists have asked for the ability to access the details of the lists:

 

Auditors

To audit and verify that the correct watch lists are in use

 

Compliance managers

To assess the completeness and coverage of screening content and verify their custom lists post-upload

 

Screening specialists

For support during an investigation to understand why certain names are flagged and pre-emptively analyse watch list contents

 

Here again, to help customers, a new Fiori app has been released: Display Watch Lists

With this app users can display all entries of the watch lists they are using:

 

 

Link back to original watch list source

 

Finally, and to close this blog on investigation by screening specialists, when a hit is generated, these experts need to verify if the associated entity is truly the one listed on the sanction party list. To do so, they often need to refer back to the original documentation – list from OFAC, United Nations, European Union, country specific… and many more! – where all attributes and comments are detailed, to ensure they enforce the right decision.

With the new capability to link back to the original watch list source, this step of the investigation is streamlined and handling time per alert due to manual searching reduced:

What about you, how does your company address these needs of constantly changing sanction lists and complex investigations? I look forward to reading your thoughts and comments on this blog.

And if you are interested in learning more about SAP solutions for Governance, Risk, and Compliance, feel free to fill-in the demo request form!

entry.title: Re: Budget Availability Control for WBS Element in PR/PO...
entry.content:
Some of the options / Best Practices in Public Cloud

1) Custom Logic (BAdI)
You can implement a Business Add‑In (BAdI) in the Purchase Requisition or Purchase Order creation process. Logic can check the WBS budget consumption and raise a warning or error message. This is the closest equivalent to AVC in public cloud.

2) Workflow‑based Validation
Configure a workflow approval step that triggers when PR/PO exceeds budget. This does not block creation but prevents release until approved.

3) Alternative Objects (Internal Orders)
If strict budget control is required, some customers use internal orders with budget checks (supported in cloud). Internal orders can enforce budget availability more tightly than WBS in public cloud.

entry.title: Re: My Purchase Requisition becomes Advance license
entry.content:
@jozdzinski I am really happy to hear that 🙂

Yes, you are right, these changes can be confusing and difficult to understand.

During my IAM training from SAP Consulting I was told that the smartest way is to not add all dependent apps. Instead, I am always starting with just the specific app I need and then run a test if the process works as expected.

  • If yes, then I am happy and I do not add the additional apps.
  • If no, then I perform an analysis (by checking via app “Display Authorization Audit”) and add only the dependent app that I am specifically missing.

 

Such changes can be tracked in advance by using tools like the What’s New Viewer (What's New Viewer - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition | SAP Help Portal) or RASD (Release Assessment and Scope Dependency for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition) as described here: Key Tools and Resources for IAM Upgrade & Change Management | SAP Help Portal.

However, I have felt in recent releases that I can not find all changes here. So it’s best to just go through the system with heightened sensed in the weeks after a release.

Best regards,

Kerisha

entry.title: Wiosenne Spotkanie Katowickiej Grupy SAP Community - Kwiecień 2026
entry.content:
Relacja ze spotkania SAP Stammtisch – Katowice, 15.04.2026

Kwietniowe spotkanie katowickiego SAP Stammtisch po raz kolejny pokazało, że siła tej inicjatywy tkwi w ludziach i otwartej wymianie doświadczeń. 15 kwietnia spotkaliśmy się w sprawdzonym gronie, ale co szczególnie cieszy – dołączyły do nas również trzy nowe osoby, które od razu wniosły świeżą perspektywę i energię do dyskusji.

Już od pierwszych minut było widać, że nie będzie to „zwykłe” spotkanie. Rozmowy szybko zeszły na konkretne, techniczne tematy – od podejścia do clean code w projektach SAP, przez praktyczne doświadczenia z SAP BTP, aż po realne wyzwania, które pojawiają się podczas wdrożeń. To właśnie ten miks - teorii, praktyki i szczerych opinii z projektów - sprawia, że Stammtisch ma swoją unikalną wartość.

Nie zabrakło również dyskusji o tym, co zmienia się w świecie SAP na bieżąco. Uczestnicy dzielili się obserwacjami dotyczącymi rozwoju produktów, zmian w podejściu do architektury oraz kierunków, w jakich zmierza ekosystem SAP. Takie rozmowy często wykraczają poza „oficjalną dokumentację” i pozwalają spojrzeć na technologie z zupełnie innej, bardziej praktycznej strony.

Szczególnie wartościowy był fakt, że nowe osoby nie tylko słuchały, ale aktywnie uczestniczyły w rozmowach zadając pytania, dzieląc się własnymi doświadczeniami i wnosząc nowe wątki. To najlepszy dowód na to, że SAP Stammtisch jest miejscem otwartym niezależnie od poziomu doświadczenia czy roli w projektach, gdzie wszyscy uczestnicy chętnie ze sobą rozmawiają.

Co dalej?

Nie zwalniamy tempa! Kolejne spotkanie zapowiada się równie ciekawie i odbędzie się już 24.06.2026.

Planujemy kontynuować tematykę AI w SAP, ze szczególnym naciskiem na SAP Joule, który budzi coraz większe zainteresowanie i rodzi sporo pytań w kontekście praktycznego wykorzystania.

Dodatkowo na tapetę trafi bardzo aktualny temat:

  • zmiany w API Policy (v4 2026) i ich wpływ na istniejące integracje klientów

To zagadnienie już teraz wywołuje sporo dyskusji w społeczności i z pewnością będzie okazją do wymiany konkretnych doświadczeń oraz potencjalnych strategii dostosowania się do nowych zasad.

Dlaczego warto przyjść?

Bo to nie jest konferencja ani webinar trzymający się sztywnych ram i wąskiej tematyki.
To miejsce, gdzie:

  • możesz swobodnie pogadać o realnych problemach projektowych
  • poznasz ludzi z różnych obszarów SAP
  • dowiesz się, jak inni rozwiązują podobne wyzwania
  • i co najważniejsze, poszerzysz nowe kontakty

Każde kolejne spotkanie pokazuje, że społeczność rośnie, a wraz z nią rośnie poziom i różnorodność dyskusji.

Jeśli jeszcze nie byłeś – to dobry moment, żeby dołączyć.
Jeśli byłeś – wiesz, że warto wrócić! 🙂
